‚Äéwin the lottery jackpot that would enable me to rise above them ALL! I was so depressed that even when my mother came up to Santa Barbara with my sister and her friends for a short day trip
column2: I refused to see them. For the next month
column3: I barely left my room. I was completely and utterly at the end of all hope. My life is over
column4: I thought. Without that wealth
column5: what was there to live for in the future? I still couldn‚Äôt believe I didn‚Äôt win. I kept thinking about the heavenly life I would be living if I had won. I was certain of my victory
column6: right at the moment of the drawing. Instead
column7: it turned to a crushing defeat
column8: just like everything else in my life. Everything I had tried to do in the past
column9: ever since childhood
column10: had been a failure. It was very hard to feel good about myself anymore. I spent all of my time drifting aimlessly
column11: doing nothing with my time except brooding over my fate. I didn‚Äôt want to think about anything. I could barely breathe from the stifling loneliness. All of my energy had been sapped out of me. In the month of April
column12: James Ellis officially ended the friendship between us. James hadn‚Äôt contacted me at all since the Lemelson‚Äôs Christmas party
column13: and I felt extremely offended by this. For the first few months of 2012
column14: I had been trying to contact him
column15: demanding to know why he continued to act so cold and distant to me. I thought that after we spoke with each other at the Lemelson‚Äôs
column16: things would get better between us. I was dead wrong. I got hold of him on the phone in February
column17: and he said a few words to me before quickly making an excuse to hang up. A month later I messaged him on Facebook to tell him how rude he was on the phone
column18: and in April I received a response from him. He blatantly said he didn‚Äôt want to be friends anymore. He didn‚Äôt even deign to tell me why. After he said the fateful words
column19: he refused to talk to me ever again. That was the last time I ever spoke to him. It was the ultimate betrayal. I thought he was the one friend I had in the whole world who truly understood me
column20: who truly understood my views and the reasons why I thought the way I did about the world. I confided everything to him
column21: because I thought we were on the same page. To be betrayed in such a manner wounded me deeply
column22: though I never admitted it to anyone. On the day of the betrayal
column23: I thought back on our entire friendship. James Ellis was my oldest friend. I remembered the first time I met him
column24: as we kicked dust together as First Graders at Topanga Elementary school. I remembered all of the good times I spent at his various houses in the Palisades
column25: trading Pokemon cards when we were little

column2: time is fucking flying

column4: and the collective IQ of the american collective declining. I will not give exact details
column5: but a mindfuck IRL happened. I quickly JIDF'd a lot of my troll dust

column10: an underwater city (not worked on)
column11: along with other projects. This was a game that was pretty much abandoned (Besides a small group of people) since 2008
column12: revived with some activity after nearly a decade of rot. I feel blessed to have experienced some fresh memories of the game. I'm considering maybe making a blog about the game but I know for a fact nobody would visit it. Hell

column14: but oh well. I'm not trying to talk to myself; I'm trying to document my experiences
column15: and to give some words of wisdom to you. I've been neglecting some of my most important work

column18: even if some of the mods and admins on it are autistic cunts. But

column1: ‚ÄéWhile we were eating
column2: some high school kids walked in. James saw them first
column3: and right when he saw them he said the words ‚ÄúWe‚Äôre fucked‚Äù. James knew I would have trouble with them. They were popular boys who had a flock of pretty girls with them. One of them sat down with two of the girls
column4: putting his leg up on another chair with a cocky smirk on his face. I was livid with rage
column5: and I wanted to pour my drink all over his head. James knew exactly what I was planning to do; we had been through similar incidents before. He made a lot of effort to try to dissuade me from acting on my anger
column6: pointing out that there was a security guard nearby. I did the only other thing I could do; I packed up my dinner and left the restaurant
column7: fleeing in defeat and shame. James soon followed
column8: and we decided to finish our meal at his house. A dark and ominous aura clouded over our friendship that day. When the two of us got back to James‚Äôs house
column9: I was still seething with rage. I didn‚Äôt understand why James wasn‚Äôt angry like me. The sight that we just witnessed was horrible to watch. To see another male be successful with females is torture for males like us who have no success with females. I was so angry that I told James of all of the acts of revenge I wanted to exact on those popular boys. I told him my desire to flay them alive
column10: to strip the skins off their flesh and make them scream in agony as punishment for living a better life than me. James became deeply disturbed by my anger. I wished that he wasn‚Äôt disturbed. I wished he could be a friend that felt the same way about the world that I did. But he wasn‚Äôt that kind of person. He was a weakling. Once I had calmed down
column11: the two of us had a long conversation in his room
column12: and I ended up crying in front of him as I explained how hopeless I felt about life. Soon after that
column13: I left his house
column14: never to return there again. He will never invite me over after that incident
column15: and our friendship will slowly fade to dust. During the last few days that I had to endure living with those barbaric housemates
column16: I often walked out to Isla Vista hoping that I could meet a girl and take her home with me. I wanted to prove to them all that girls liked me
column17: to see the look on their faces when they see a girl by my side. But of course
column18: I had nothing to prove because girls didn‚Äôt like me. Every time I tried to go out and meet a girl
column19: I ended up walking home alone in anger. On one of these nights
column20: I crossed paths with a boy who was walking with two pretty girls. I got so envious that I cursed at them

‚Äéwas going to Pinecrest was a geeky kid named Nate Grossman
column2: who I didn‚Äôt really interact with that much in Topanga. I also felt an intense fear of what Middle School life would be like. I didn‚Äôt know how to act around girls
column3: I didn‚Äôt know what was cool anymore
column4: I had no friends there. I simply didn‚Äôt know what to do. I felt like I was walking into a snowstorm without a coat. My parents led me into the school to say goodbye
column5: and then it was time for me to start my first class. I had to take multiple classes with different teachers now. This was also a new concept for me and it made me extremely uncomfortable. Since this was a private school
column6: I had to wear a uniform
column7: something I hadn‚Äôt done since going to Dorsett House in England. I thought of this as a good thing though... I didn‚Äôt have to worry about what I would have to wear on the first day. For the first few days
column8: I withdrew into a defensive shell and didn‚Äôt really talk to anyone. I did observe
column9: however. I observed how everyone acted
column10: who the ‚Äúcool kids‚Äù were
column11: what they were like... and it was all so intimidating. The social challenges that I faced in Fifth Grade were intensified tenfold. I noticed that there were two groups of cool
column12: popular kids. There were the skateboarder kids
column13: such as Vinny Maggio
column14: Ashton Moio
column15: Darrel
column16: Wes
column17: and Alex Dib. And then there were the boys who were popular with girls
column18: including Vincent
column19: Robert Morgan
column20: and Oren Aks. They all seemed so confident and aggressive. I felt so intimidated by them
column21: and I hated them for it. I hated them so much
column22: but I had to increase my standing with them. I wanted to be friends with them. I also observed the girls. I was still very short for my age
column23: and most of the girls were taller than me. I hadn‚Äôt reached puberty yet
column24: but I was starting to admire female prettiness. There was one group of pretty
column25: popular girls
column26: and they all seemed to like hanging out with that boy Robert Morgan. I didn‚Äôt yet desire girls sexually
column27: but I still felt envy towards Robert for being able to attract the attention of all the popular girls. What was so special about Robert Morgan? I constantly asked myself. I thought all of the cool kids were obnoxious jerks
column28: but I tried as best as I could to hide my disgust and appear ‚Äúcool‚Äù to them. They were obnoxious jerks
column29: and yet somehow it was these boys who all of the girls flocked to. This showed me that the world was a brutal place
column30: and human beings were nothing more than savage animals. Everything my father taught me was proven wrong. He raised me to be a polite
column31: kind gentleman. In a decent world
column32: that would be ideal. But the polite
column33: kind gentleman doesn‚Äôt win in the real world. The girls don‚Äôt flock to the gentlemen. They flock to the alpha male.‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

2003-12-29‚Äéof ""electric"" feeling
column2: Max could smell it as well as feel it slightly. Mixed in with the scent of the rain that continued to poor as if God was crying was barely noticeable. Max closed his eye's
column3: thinking of his house. His nice little house on Gorman ST where he and his mom and dad lived
column4: and his dog Chopper. He wanted to be home
column5: in bed
column6: he wanted to wake up and find that this had all been a bad dream. He wanted to wake up and find that this had all been an elaborate nightmare
column7: cooked up by his subconscious from eating the wrong thing's and watching horror movies before bed. He pinched himself to reassure himself that this wasn't a dream
column8: a nightmare
column9: but reality. Cold harsh merciless reality. He pondered on his loved ones
column10: and his friend's he hadn't seen since the school that morning
column11: wondering if they were ok. He began to doubt that he would ever see them again
column12: that he would ever wake up Saturday morning's to the smell of his is mother making breakfast
column13: since she never had the time during week day's because of work. Saturday's were always special
column14: more tear's began to well up in his eye's as he realized he would never see another Saturday morning. Morticia had the same feeling's
column15: she wanted to be home
column16: listening to music and drawing as she usually did out of boredom. She wanted to hear her Evanescence CD once more
column17: she had forgotten it that morning on her dresser. But it wouldn't have been of much good use
column18: since her CD player ran out of battery's that morning. She wanted to see her cat again
column19: she wanted to hold Boot's once more and pet him. Hear and feel him purring in her arm's affectionately. She wanted to argue with her mother about thing's that weren't really important
column20: only now had she come to the realization that she and her mother never really talked. Arguing was their form of communication
column21: the argument's were never anger filled
column22: they were the only way the two knew how to talk. She realized now
column23: now that it was too late
column24: that she wanted to have a regular conversation with her mother. She lowered her head in sadness
column25: knowing that that would never happen. ""Morticia...

column2: I spent a lot of time meditating about how I would deal with this huge change. I had to prepare myself as much as possible
column3: so I did a lot of introspecting and evaluated myself in great detail. This move to Santa Barbara was the only chance I had of attaining the life I desire. I had to do my best to make this work
column4: proclaiming to myself that this time
column5: I will not fail. I exercised in the gym for many hours to boost my confidence as much as possible
column6: and I went to the mall to shop for clothes. Last Christmas I got a few gift cards for Macy‚Äôs
column7: and I spent them all on a few shirts that I thought I would look good in
column8: as well as new shoes. After doing everything I could do to physically boost my confidence and appearance
column9: I was ready. And so ends another era of my extraordinary and tragic life. I call it the era of Hope and Hopelessness
column10: where I drifted and languished in lonely despair while I lived at my mother‚Äôs apartment and attended two colleges. At various intervals
column11: something happened to give me a new hope for my life
column12: only to have it shattered later on. My life had been moving in that same pattern for a long time now
column13: and I was sick and tired of it. All while I was suffering this lonely existence
column14: other boys my age lived their happy lives of pleasure and sex. I can never forgive such an injustice
column15: and it was my bid to overcompensate for it in the future. I had to make up for all the years I lost in loneliness and isolation
column16: through no fault of my own! It was society‚Äôs fault for rejecting me. It was women‚Äôs fault for refusing to have sex with me. The move to Santa Barbara is the endgame
column17: the ultimate climax of everything. I saw it as a new chance that was given to me to finally have the things I want in life: love
column18: sex
column19: friends
column20: fun
column21: acceptance
column22: a sense of belonging. But I could never forgive the world for denying me such things in the past. I was already turning twenty soon. I had already lost many years of my life. I deserve better than that. I am an intelligent gentleman
column23: and I deserve the love of girls more than the other obnoxious boys of my age
column24: and yet they get girls and I don‚Äôt. That is a crime that can never be forgotten
column25: nor can it be forgiven. I always wanted to exact my revenge on humanity for forcing me to live such a life
column26: but I‚Äôve also always had the hope that if I can do things in life to make up for all my suffering
column27: then that in itself would be a form of peaceful revenge. In truth

column1: ‚Äéthe floor to examine what we got. That was my favorite part of it. Early in my Third Grade year
column2: my mother would often take us to a festival near Topanga Canyon Boulevard
column3: where small concerts were held and people barbequed great food. A friend of hers had something to do with these events
column4: and I played with the son of this friend. He was named Riley Anapol
column5: and he was two years younger than me. A First Grader. I played with some other younger kids there as well
column6: peers of Riley
column7: and I had a good time. Riley became a common friend for a while. The significance of this is that Riley Anapol would eventually become someone I would harbor a great hatred for. Riley would grow up to get lots of girls
column8: and I would grow up to be rejected by girls. But back then he was a friend
column9: a peer
column10: and we were playing together as equals. It‚Äôs funny how the world works. When the holidays arrived
column11: my father announced that we were going to take a family vacation to Soumaya‚Äôs home country of Morocco and meet her family there
column12: and afterwards we were to stop by in England. I wasn‚Äôt excited about Morocco

column1: 2011-12-16‚Äé"Today I dreamt a Columbine reference for the first time. I've probably never had one before because I've scarcely ever cared about Columbine. I began my dream by waking up at age 14 in my extremely dark bedroom during a power outage. I knew that there were ghoulies outside my locked door
column2: roaming around the rest of the house. I tried to get my pocket flashlight to work
column3: but it wasn't consistently staying on. Replacement batteries didn't help
column4: so I instead tried using my backup flashlight: a partially-filled roll of dimes. I spent a while fumbling around with loose dimes
column5: trying to fill up the roll
column6: and eventually ""my brother"" came up to the other side of the locked door. He told me that he needed my help with the ghoulies. I was saying
column7: ""Oh
column8: fuck off. I know you're not my brother. You yourself are one of the ghoulies""
column9: as I was continuing to fumble around trying to fill the roll of dimes. I ended up not having enough dimes to entirely fill it
column10: but at least I had enough for it to give off a dim ray of light. I got out of bed and prepared for a fight with the ghoulies. I went downstairs as my flashlight dream- vanished
column11: but at this point the rest of the house was dimly lit anyway. As I was about to go into the kitchen
column12: a huge purple monster with long arms leapt forth from around a corner and pinned me against a wall. It sort of looked like this: http://www.raukusbaukus.com/images/grap ... ilcher.jpg I realized that it was some student I knew when I was 12. He tried talking to me
column13: and basically said something to the extent of
column14: ""Everyone picked on me for being a huge purple monster
column15: and so now I'm going to go Columbine. I already went to a bunch of other kids' houses to find a partner
column16: but they refused my offer
column17: so I ate them. You're next on the list. Do you want to join me?"". The way it apparently worked was that I could get bitten by him and then choose which form I wanted to be in at will. I thought
column18: why not get bitten by him so I could become a cool purple monster whenever I pleased
column19: and then just use my new monster powers to kill him right after he bites me? So I was like
column20: ""Sure
column21: bro"". He kept trying to bite different parts of me
column22: but it always ended in me whining
column23: ""Ooowwwwww""
column24: he would let go
column25: look at the spot
column26: and say
column27: ""I didn't bite long enough! It didn't work"". We went through this for quite a while
column28: and then I had the thought that
column29: ""You shouldn't just bite me -anywhere-. It should be high up on my arm so people don't see the mark
column30: the same reason why doctors inject patients on their arm (dream logic). He rolled up my sleeve
column31: pointed at my vein
column32: and I said
column33: ""No! Doctors would find out because they inject there
column34: of course!‚ÄéAdam Lanza‚Äé1

column3: but then again I wouldn‚Äôt even call it life. The existence I‚Äôve had on this world during the last eight years is anything but life. A feeling of overwhelming dizziness and anxiety swept over me. I was actually going to die. I couldn‚Äôt believe it. Then I realized that my life was already over anyway. I was never going to lose my virginity; I was never going to experience love and sex; I was never going to have children. This final act of Retribution is the only thing I could do. column4: opulence
column5: and prestige; and traveling on Virgin Atlantic Upper Class would give me that experience

column4: which is why they told me last minute. The last day of mother‚Äôs week was the day before we would depart. Mother took me and my sister to a barbeque at the house of her friends Alan and Rebecca. I was very sad for the whole day. I didn‚Äôt do anything at the barbeque except swing on their swing in misery. When we got home I played WoW for the very last time. I took advantage of the few hours I had left on it. My mother allowed me to stay up until midnight playing. I acquired a very nice piece of armor for my character. I didn‚Äôt want to leave it. When we arrived at father‚Äôs house the next day
column5: I heard even more upsetting news. Father had to work for the first few weeks of summer

column12: again in Paris

column16: and she was already settled in. After unpacking at Khadija‚Äôs house
column17: we walked to Soumaya‚Äôs father‚Äôs huge house where I met Ayman again. I remembered playing with Ayman on my last trip to Morocco... He grew up a lot since then. To my dismay
column18: he was taller and stronger than me
column19: despite being two years younger. I was always short and physically weak... that‚Äôs how it‚Äôs been all my life. We instantly became friends again after catching up a little
column20: and I played hide-and- seek tag with him and his two younger brothers. I disliked having to be in Morocco for the whole summer
column21: but I tried to make the best of it. Ayman made the time I spent there more fun. We often went out by ourselves to explore the city of Tangier. Ayman knew where everything was
column22: and Soumaya trusted him to show me around. Georgia sometimes came with us. We‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

column2: I looked at the Powerball again
column3: and saw that the jackpot had risen to over $100 million. This prompted me to drive to Arizona again
column4: in another desperate attempt to become instantly wealthy so that I could attract beautiful girls and live the life I want. After all of the rejection and mistreatment I‚Äôve experienced at the hands of women
column5: I knew that becoming wealthy was the only way I could become worthy of them
column6: and so my obsession with becoming wealthy at a young age came back in full force for the first few months of 2013. By the month of April
column7: I had driven to Arizona three more times
column8: making a total of four trips to Arizona in my lifetime
column9: just to buy lottery tickets out of intense desperation
column10: believing it to be my only hope of attaining the life I desire
column11: the life I know I‚Äôm worthy of. I kept dreaming of the life I would have once I won; the beautiful blonde girlfriend
column12: the luxurious mansion with a magnificent view
column13: all of the exotic cars I would drive to impress girls. It gave me hope. It gave me something to live for. That hope was shattered after each attempt. None of the tickets I bought on those trips fulfilled my dreams. The reason I kept going
column14: even after I didn‚Äôt win
column15: was because I truly believed I was supposed to win. I wanted to believe it because I wanted something to live for. I needed to have hope. I knew that if I lost all of my hope
column16: I would have nothing to live for but revenge. Any chance of having a happy life would be doomed. At the end of March
column17: when I checked my last set of tickets that I had bought from my last trip to Arizona
column18: and saw that I didn‚Äôt win
column19: any hope I had of becoming wealthy at a young age was finally and indefinitely shattered. It fully dawned on me that the life I had envisioned for myself would never come to pass. The children I would have in the future with a beautiful blonde girlfriend ceased to exist
column20: as if they were murdered. There won‚Äôt be any beautiful blonde girlfriend for me now. No girl would be my girlfriend unless I had great wealth... I learned that from my life of being rejected. I was doomed to a life of lonely virginity. In April
column21: the Powerball lottery was introduced in the state of California
column22: so if I ever wanted to play it again I would no longer have to drive to Arizona. However
column23: I was so shaken by not winning in the last few months that I gave up on it for a while
column24: but eventually I would have episodes of little surges of hope in which I‚Äôd buy a few tickets
column25: just to have something to hope for as I endure the last few months of my torturous life. All of those little‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

column1: ‚Äéjoking about how lame Pokemon players were
column2: and I decided it was time to quit. I talked to James about this. He was still interested in Pokemon
column3: so I gave him my Charizard card as a gift
column4: and as an act of my resignation from the game. Pokemon gave me some really happy and memorable experiences
column5: but it was time to move on. I then started to notice that all of the cool kids were interested in skateboarding. I had never even ridden on a skateboard before
column6: but if I wanted to be cool
column7: I had to become a skateboarder. I expressed this to my parents
column8: and my father was glad that I was showing an interest in an active sport. He took me to the store Val Surf on Ventura Boulevard to buy me a new skateboard
column9: and I was fascinated by all of the different choices. I settled for a red Val Surf branded Skateboard
column10: and they took it down from the wall and built it for me. I was thrilled to have this new skateboard and the possible chance it gave me to be a cool kid. It was time to start practicing. I found it very hard to even ride on it in the beginning
column11: and I spent many hours outside trying to get the hang of it. And that was that
column12: I was now a skateboarder
column13: though not yet good enough to reveal myself as one to the kids at school. This was the start of an obsession to copy everything the supposed ‚Äúcool kids‚Äù were doing. Part 3 The Last Period of Contentment Age 9-13 Fourth grade ended
column14: and once the summer started
column15: I took a vow to mold myself into the coolest kid I could possibly be by the time Fifth grade began. I anticipated the approval the other cool kids would have of me once I reveal myself as being similar to them
column16: and I looked forward to it. After about a year and a half of living in the house on Hatteras St. in upper West Hills
column17: my father decided to move into an even better house. This time
column18: all of us spent a day looking at open houses together as a family. We went with a real estate agent and examined some beautiful homes around Woodland Hills. My favorite one was a 3-story house on Llano Drive
column19: in the Woodland Hills Heights
column20: the most prestigious area of Woodland Hills that bordered Calabasas. It didn‚Äôt have a pool
column21: but it had a sloping backyard almost three-times as large as our current one. The house had six bedrooms
column22: and I took an intense liking to one particular bedroom that had its own bathroom and a personal balcony. My father showed extreme enthusiasm about possibly buying this house
column23: and I became obsessed with getting that particular bedroom as my own room. When I brought it up with father and Soumaya
column24: they said that the room would most likely be Georgia‚Äôs because it was closer to the master bedroom. They said that I would get‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

‚Äéwere staying in it temporarily. Mother took us there a few times where I adventured with James in the wilderness area that surrounded the house. We would often go to a small plaza in the center of Malibu. There was a playground there
column2: with a few shops and restaurants surrounding it. It was time for my 11th birthday. I was at mother‚Äôs house and just decided to have a small playdate for my birthday. I invited James over
column3: along with another kid who I had befriended at the Woodland Hills recreation center. My mother made a small cake
column4: I blew out the candles
column5: and that was it. I was eleven years old. 11 Years Old The trip to France and England began shortly after my birthday. We had been talking about it for a while at father‚Äôs house
column6: and I was really excited to go. We traveled on Virgin Atlantic Upper Class. I was extremely enthusiastic about this
column7: as I always loved luxury and opulence. We stopped by in England for a couple of days to say hello to grandma Jinx. The cousins weren‚Äôt there
column8: they were already in France
column9: so it was a bit boring. When we arrived in France
column10: the feeling of wonder and curiosity swept over me as it always did when I visited a foreign country. The last time I was in France
column11: I was only a few weeks old. This was the first time I was able to truly experience the country. France was a whole different world
column12: and it was a world that I liked. French culture is so exquisite and refined compared to American culture. After booking a couple of rooms at a small Inn near the town of Toulouse
column13: we met up with Jonny and the cousin‚Äôs at Aunt Jenny‚Äôs house. Aunt Jenny is my father‚Äôs sister
column14: and the last time I saw her was when I lived in England
column15: before the move to America. She had a few kittens in her house that I loved to play with. George and I immediately resumed our friendship that started in the spring. There was a vast forested area surrounding the house. George told me there were lots of wild boars in the forest
column16: so we went ‚Äúwild boar hunting‚Äù. It was just a game
column17: and we never ended up seeing any boars at all
column18: but the suspense of possibly finding one was what made it fun. We stayed in Toulouse for about a week
column19: and then we said goodbye to the relatives and set off to tour the country. We toured many cultural towns and stayed in castle-style hotels. This should have been a great experience for me
column20: but my conflicts with Soumaya soured it. There were a few incidents in which she punished me by making me stay in my hotel room while she
column21: father and Georgia all went out to dinner at a restaurant. I hated her for this. On the way back
column22: we stopped at grandma Jinx‚Äôs house in England for a week. The cousins were there this time

column2: and I was slowly starting to realize it. When I became aware of this common social structure at my school

column4: with some horror
column5: that I wasn‚Äôt ‚Äúcool‚Äù at all. I had a dorky hairstyle
column6: I wore plain and uncool clothing
column7: and I was shy and unpopular. I was always described as the shy boy in the past
column8: but I never really thought my shyness would affect me in a negative way
column9: until this point. This revelation about the world
column10: and about myself
column11: really decreased my self-esteem. On top of this was the feeling that I was different because I am of mixed race. I am half White
column12: half Asian
column13: and this made me different from the normal fully-white kids that I was trying to fit in with. I envied the cool kids
column14: and I wanted to be one of them. I was a bit frustrated at my parents for not shaping me into one of these kids in the past. They never made an effort to dress me in stylish clothing or get me a good-looking haircut. I had to make every effort to rectify this. I had to adapt. column21: and he came up to me and patted my head

‚Äéhis friend Karl Champley for a few weeks
column2: to help him build a staircase in his new house. I knew Karl quite well
column3: for he used to come over to father‚Äôs for dinner occasionally. Karl was just finishing up building his new house in Woodland Hills
column4: just a few minutes away from father‚Äôs house
column5: and he offered to hire me to help with the staircase. I agreed to take this job. Sure
column6: construction work was lowly and laborious
column7: but this was different. This was more like assisting a friend
column8: and it would be in a private environment. It was the perfect temporary job opportunity
column9: and it would most definitely get my mother off my back. I still wasn‚Äôt able to drive
column10: so I rode my bicycle there from mother‚Äôs house every morning. The trip on the bicycle took 30 minutes. It was grueling to ride a bicycle up that steep winding road every day
column11: but it provided good exercise
column12: which I was in need of. I worked with Karl every weekday for about three weeks. It turned out to be quite a pleasant experience. Karl was very friendly and I enjoyed working with him. When we finished the staircase
column13: which was a spiral staircase that led up to his roof-deck
column14: we took a moment to admire the work we did. On my last day working for Karl
column15: I decided to stop by at father‚Äôs house to have a drink. I was quite parched from the bicycle ride. I entered the house without knocking because I believed I had the right to. As the eldest son
column16: the house should be my house after my father. Soumaya was surprised to see me
column17: and she got angry that I didn‚Äôt knock. To teach me a lesson
column18: she ordered me to go back outside and knock. I refused
column19: telling her that she has no right to order me around anymore. I then helped myself to a glass of water. Soumaya knocked the glass of water out of my hand and it shattered on the floor. Father clamored angrily up the stairs from his office demanding to know what was going on. The three of us had a heated argument
column20: and of course father took Soumaya‚Äôs side. They both kicked me out of the house
column21: telling me that I‚Äôm not to return. I felt betrayed and humiliated as I furiously made my way back to mother‚Äôs house. At that very moment
column22: I hated both of them
column23: and I wouldn‚Äôt see either of them for many months. For those months
column24: my father was dead to me. My mother was all I had left in this bleak world. During that same week
column25: I had a climactic meeting with Philip and Addison where my noxious feud with Addison Altendorf reached a boiling point. We went on an outing to the Griffith Park Observatory
column26: as we usually did when we got together. This time
column27: my arguments with Addison were very intense. I tried to insult him as much as I could
column28: in a petty attempt to get revenge at him‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

‚Äécame over for a sleepover. We would play Nintendo 64 games like Tony Hawk‚Äôs Pro Skater and Donkey Kong late into the night
column2: and then on Sunday morning mother would take us both to Skatelab
column3: an indoor skatepark in Simi Valley. James had become really interested in skateboarding too
column4: or so I believed. I was always better at it than him though
column5: and I liked it that way. This was the way every weekend went during mother‚Äôs week
column6: and I had the time of my life. I was so interested in skateboarding that I took my skateboard trick-or-treating for Halloween. My costume
column7: of course
column8: was myself as a skateboarder. We went to the Lemelson‚Äôs for a nice dinner and then set out to collect our candy. It was quite tricky to hold a bag full of candy while skateboarding
column9: but I had fun. I remember some teenagers seeing me on my skateboard and saying ‚Äúwhy didn‚Äôt I think of that‚Äù. Hah
column10: that was gratifying. For Christmas
column11: my mother bought me the new Playstation 2. I had been wanting it for a long time
column12: and when I unwrapped the present and saw the box
column13: I felt so elated. Beforehand
column14: the only video game console I played was the Nintendo 64 (and the Gameboy
column15: if that counts). The Playstation 2 was much more advanced in graphics
column16: and it amazed me. When mother announced that I would have to share it with my sister Georgia and that I can‚Äôt keep it in my room
column17: my excitement turned to indignation
column18: and I threw quite a tantrum. After crying for a bit
column19: I calmed down and settled to sharing it with Georgia. She wouldn‚Äôt be using it much anyways
column20: I told myself. Even after getting a Playstation 2
column21: I still played my Nintendo 64 a lot because I loved the games I had for it
column22: and I had an emotional attachment to it. The Nintendo 64 was the first video game console I played
column23: and it would always have a special place in my heart. One day during winter break at father‚Äôs house
column24: father and Soumaya went out for a few hours and left me and my sister with Tracy. When they came back
column25: they had a little puppy with them
column26: and announced that it was our new pet. It was mainly a present for Georgia. Georgia had been desperately asking father for a pet puppy for the last year
column27: but I didn‚Äôt think he would actually go through with it. I was so shocked that we now had a dog. I was always afraid of dogs when I was little
column28: and I never imagined having one as a pet. The only pets I‚Äôve had previously were my turtle and iguana
column29: who both died within a year of acquiring them. Georgia was given the choice on what to name the puppy
column30: and she named it Lucky. I thought this was a very lame and stupid name. When I returned to school after the winter break
column31: I noticed that all the cool kids had another interest: Hacky‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

column2: if only he were still my friend. On New Year‚Äôs Eve
column3: I stayed at my mother‚Äôs house
column4: feeling lonely and miserable. I believe father and Soumaya went to Antje Twinn‚Äôs house again. There was no way I would go there again after the incident that occurred the year before. I found it quite hilarious that everything during this holiday was playing out just the same way as it played out in the previous year; the trip to England
column5: father and Soumaya attending the same party at the Twinn‚Äôs house
column6: my same miserable
column7: lonely
column8: virgin status. Indeed
column9: my life was moving in vicious circle of torment with no way out. The only thing that got me through it all was my hope that there was a way out
column10: and that hope had been shattered too many times. I couldn‚Äôt live like this anymore. I knew that if things continue to be the same
column11: I would have to carry out my ultimate Retribution and die in the process. I didn‚Äôt want to die. I fear death
column12: but death is better than living such a miserable
column13: insignificant life. I remained in my hometown for a couple of weeks
column14: and then I went back to Santa Barbara
column15: the place of beauty and romance that I‚Äôve had to suffer in lonely hell. I only signed up for online classes for the Spring semester
column16: but that was only to placate my parents. I didn‚Äôt see the point in even bothering with college anymore. Having to walk through SBCC with all of those beautiful girls strutting around in their revealing shorts
column17: showing off their sexy legs... It is torture
column18: because I know that they would all reject me. There are so many beautiful girls in Santa Barbara
column19: but not one of them ever wanted to be my girlfriend. Life would have been so perfect there if only girls were attracted to me. For the month of January 2013
column20: I stayed in my room all the time
column21: assessing my situation. I brooded and contemplated about everything. I could hear all of those happy young people partying and enjoying themselves as the new semester came to a start. I mused that I should be out there
column22: experiencing a happy
column23: healthy college life with a group of friends
column24: meeting up with girls and sleeping with them
column25: just like all other guys did in college. I thought about how it was almost two years since I had moved to Santa Barbara
column26: and that only made my rage and frustration grow. As I listened to all of those young people having so much fun right outside my window
column27: my hatred of them all for not accepting me only festered and boiled inside me
column28: filling me with immense pain. At one point
column29: I looked out my window and saw couples strolling around the street
column30: on their way to some party. They probably slept with each other that night. The sight made me feel so inferior
column31: like a little mouse. I felt like I was at the bottom of the food chain.‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

2011-11-08‚Äé"The guy who wrote Pumped Up Kicks said that the song is about an outcast teenager losing his mind while plotting revenge without any explicit violence occurring. People have interpreted that to mean that it's about a school shooting. Because of the name Robert
column2: Robert Hawkins has now become a stereotypical poster child of school shootings. If any name other than ""Robert"" had been used for the song
column3: no one would have associated Robert Hawkins with Pumped Up Kicks. If the name ""Robert"" had still been in the song
column4: but Robert Hawkins had actually been 32 years old (the average age of American mass murderers in the last 20 years)
column5: again
column6: no one would have associated him with Pumped Up Kicks. They would have instead said that ""Pumped Up Kicks is surely about Robert Steinhauser""
column7: not at all because he fits in with it better than anyone else would have
column8: but merely because his name was ""Robert"". At least with Robert Steinhauser
column9: it almost sort of vaguely fits in with the interpretation many people have that the song is about school shootings. Robert Hawkins's mass murder unquestionably was not a school shooting: he shot up an upscale shopping store. Without applying my pet theory as to the origin of mass murder
column10: which you're accusing me of doing
column11: anyone can see that Robert Hawkins did explicitly share the socioeconomic characteristics of much older mass murderers. Instead of approaching it through simply stereotyping him as a school shooter who was bullied by his classmates
column12: it would thus be more accurate to approach it through simply stereotyping him as maybe being a young version of Mark Barton
column13: with whom he shared many similarities. http://www.independent.co.uk/news/i-don ... 09610.html If you interpret Pumped Up Kicks to be about school shootings and you think that Robert Hawkins is a better representation for a song about ""an outcast teenager losing his mind while plotting revenge without any explicit violence"" than Evan Ramsey or Michael Carneal would have been; and if you think that Pumped Up Kicks has more to do with Robert Hawkins in particular than the Omaha Shopping Mall Blues does
column14: then I don't know what I can say. It's akin to saying that I Don't Like Mondays was about Charles Whitman. I mean
column15: in a sense I guess it could be
column16: but it would be absurd to say that it's a better representation of him than it is of Brenda Spencer; and if you want to think of I Don't Like Mondays being about Charles Whitman
column17: it would be absurd to say that this song
column18: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rlBuoBV-Sa0 has less to do with Charles Whitman than I Don't Like Mondays does."‚ÄéAdam Lanza‚Äé1

‚Äéelf druid. It really blew my mind. My first experience with WoW was like stepping into another world of excitement and adventure. It was a video game world
column2: but they made it so realistic that it was like living another life
column3: a more exciting life. My life was getting more and more depressing at that point
column4: and WoW would fill in the void. It felt refreshing and relieving. I was only able to play it for a few hours for my first session. It was all I would think about when I wasn‚Äôt able to play it. Mother didn‚Äôt have a good enough computer to run World of Warcraft
column5: so I felt a bit frustrated because of that. I thought of how awesome it would be if Planet Cyber had the game
column6: but I doubted that it did. One afternoon
column7: I walked to Planet Cyber with my WoW disks and asked them if they can install my disks onto one of their computers. The owner told me the game was already being installed
column8: and I was thrilled to hear those words. It wasn‚Äôt ready yet
column9: however
column10: and I had to wait. I kept going back to Planet Cyber every day to wait for it
column11: and played other games there while they were still processing it. It was a fun wait
column12: and I knew I will eventually be able to play it. Finally
column13: after spending three days at Planet Cyber waiting
column14: it was ready. I loaded the game and logged onto my account. I was completely ecstatic. I spent all of my free time in the next few days playing it. The owner of Planet Cyber came to know me because of this series of events
column15: and he named me his best customer. I invited Charlie over
column16: and he came with Stephen
column17: an old friend from Topanga Elementary whom I hadn‚Äôt seen for a while. I found out that they both had their own WoW accounts
column18: and we went to Planet Cyber to play it together. I made a new character on their server just to play with them
column19: though I would eventually discard this character. I saw Charlie only a few more times after that. Elijah was busy with some life problems and stopped coming over. John Jo simply vanished from my life at this point
column20: for no particular reason. I can‚Äôt recall the exact last time I saw him
column21: but it was around this period. My mother decided to move to an apartment in Woodland Hills. I reacted indignantly. An apartment! I had never lived in an apartment before
column22: and I always thought of apartments as being poor and low- class. I would be embarrassed to admit it to anyone. The apartment building was called the Renaissance Apartments
column23: near the Warner Center area of Woodland Hills. We moved into a two bedroom apartment. Mother knew I was too old to share a room with my sister
column24: so she gave me the second bedroom
column25: and she and my sister shared the master bedroom. Leaving the blue house on Glade Avenue was‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

column6: and Stephen. I felt a sense of pride to be part of this group. During the daytime on this trip

column10: and barbeques. It was great fun. Nighttime in the cabin was like having a sleepover with five people
column11: and it was a new experience that excited me. Before bedtime
column12: Michael Ray took out a magazine that had pictures of beautiful model women
column13: and all of the boys gathered around and looked at them. So... even at the early age of ten
column14: boys were starting to be attracted to the female body. I didn‚Äôt understand this... I hadn‚Äôt yet reached that stage. I pretended to be interested just so that I wouldn‚Äôt appear uncool. All of those boys probably lost their virginity by sixteen. Damn them. The trip ended up being so fun that I didn‚Äôt cry at all about being away from my parents for so long. And finally
column15: it was time to graduate from Elementary School. Before the ceremony
column16: our whole class watched a video full of footage of school life throughout the year. I saw a few glimpses of myself caught in the footage
column17: and I felt gratified. My life at Topanga Elementary School was a blast
column18: full of memorable experiences and wonderful times. I dressed in a nice shirt with a tie for the ceremony. All of the Fifth Graders lined up and walked down an isle through the center of the Auditorium
column19: with the audience of parents and siblings on either side. When I saw my parents
column20: they looked so proud of me. Each student had to walk up to their teacher on the stage and receive a graduation award. We weren‚Äôt required to give a speech
column21: to my relief. I would be too nervous to talk in front of an audience. The graduation theme song was ‚ÄúTime of Your Life‚Äù by the band Green Day
column22: one of my favorite bands. Whenever I would hear this song again
column23: I would think about that glorious day
column24: and the memories would make me feel an extreme sense of nostalgia. In the afternoon
column25: there was a graduation party at the Top of Topanga community recreation center
column26: a lovely place that provided a view of the whole Valley. My mother took me to have dinner at the sushi restaurant Kabuki afterwards. It was just me and her. As we sat down at the restaurant after all the excitement
column27: I took a moment to fully ponder over the fact that Elementary School was all over. It was done. I felt so accomplished and proud... I was happy

column2: I would kill them with a hammer
column3: so they better leave me alone. I decided to delay the final decision till Monday
column4: August 24
column6: to call in the morning the secretary of judge Bishop and to ask her whether she arranged the delay of the hearing. If yes
column7: then everything was fine
column8: nothing was to be done. If no
column9: then to ask whether judge Bishop was coming to testify
column10: and again
column11: if yes
column12: that would be an indication of some respect for the due process
column13: and since I considered my defense perfect
column14: I would be prepared to face the court. If judge Bishop was not coming to court to testify
column15: that would be a clear indication that Dr. Hogben's threats are real. Even in this case I was not yet prepared to execute my plan. I appealed earlier to the university community
column16: asking them to come to the courthouse on August 25. 1 was sure that if many scientists come to support me
column17: no judge would dare to put me in jail. In the morning of August 24
column19: 1 came to my office and made a phone call to the secretary of judge Bishop. The result was worse than I expected: not only Judge Bishop was not coming
column20: his secretary did not even bother to inform him about the subpoena. It was clear to me that lawlessness ruled in court. I noticed on my desk a copy of the document granting me $1000 in travel expenses for my presentation in Haifa which was scheduled that day
column21: and which I obviously could not make. I took the document and went to the Dean's office and returned it to the budget officer. I noticed that Dean Swamy's door was closed
column22: so I asked the officer if the Dean was in
column23: and she said yes. Usually all the doors at the university were open. I knew from the past experience that whenever some dirty tricks were in the making
column24: Dean Swamy was pretending being scared
column25: hired bodyguards
column26: kept the door of his cabinet locked
column27: etc. (At my trial
column28: Dr. Swamy has admitted that I never threatened him). For me
column29: closed door of Dean Swamy's cabinet was a clear indication that some dirty tricks were in the making again
column30: and I have decided that I had no choice but to implement my plan. I went home
column31: took guns
column32: ammunition and hammer and returned back to the university. I was still very reluctant to implement the plan. I tried once again to check my computer account if there were any support messages coming from colleagues. There were none. Computer still keeps the login time at 1.34 p.m. Since for me Dean Swamy's hiding was an ominous sign
column33: I decided to check again if his door was still closed. I passed through the Dean's office. The door was closed

column6: ""Grey said ""I used to work for Murphia."" ""Murphia? whats that?
column7: ""Cibil shifted her position. ""The Irish mob...

column9: ""Cibil said. ""Heh

column11: ""Cibil put on a new shirt. ""It don't bother me

column13: ""Grey holstered his magnum. The tell tale moan of one of the creatures. ""Shit...any we barely got enough rest
column14: ""Grey stood up and walked calmly over to his shotgun. It was already loaded. He ran over to the door
column15: it shook again. He leveled the shotgun up to shoulders length and squeezed the trigger
column16: a basketball sized hole tore in the wooden door. He heard a loud thud
column17: he looked out the hole down to the headless figure below. He grinned. Grey turned to Cibil ""I just blew our hiding spot

column4: which was just down the street from Serrania Park. I wouldn‚Äôt remain at this school for long
column5: however
column6: because only weeks into my First Grade year
column7: my parents decided that they were going to move to Topanga. Most of the kids at Serrania Avenue school will end up going to Taft High School nearby
column8: a place that will cause me great suffering in the future. Perhaps some of the kids in my class at Serrania will end up turning into those who would bully me at Taft. I don‚Äôt remember any of the kids from my class there
column9: so I will never know the answer to that. It‚Äôs very disturbing to think about. I quite enjoyed my brief time at Serrania. My parents sometimes made me stay an hour after school; I believe this was because they figured it would help me make friends. I can remember this after-school playtime being a positive experience. There were always games that I played with the other kids. And thus I was a bit frustrated when my parents told me they were going to transfer me to another school after only a couple of weeks of settling into Serrania. That frustration would soon cease
column10: because the years that I would spend at Topanga Elementary school would be some of the best years of my life. The last years of being a carefree child. I started First Grade at Topanga Elementary School a couple of weeks before we prepared to move to Topanga. Topanga is a secluded
column11: mountainous community surrounding a canyon that runs through the Santa Monica Mountains

column13: when we would take trips to the beach. It has a certain rugged beauty about it. On my first day at Topanga Elementary

column15: I was going to be the ‚Äúnew kid‚Äù at school. I remember the nervousness taking over my body as my mother drove us up the steep road that led into the school proper. My new class was just lining up to start the day as we walked onto the main courtyard. My teacher
column16: Mrs. Matsuyama
column17: was very nice and understanding. My mother said goodbye and I got in line with the other students. The first kid I saw there was a chubby boy named Bryce Jacobs
column18: who was staring at me strangely. As we got to class

‚Äéthrough. When we finally did get to my situation
column2: Dr. Sophy ended up giving me the same useless advice that every other psychiatrist
column3: psychologist
column4: and counsellor had given me in the past. I don‚Äôt know why my parents wasted money on therapy
column5: as it will never help me in my struggle against such a cruel and unjust world. The doctor ended up dismissing it by prescribing me a controversial medication
column6: Risperidone. After researching this medication
column7: I found that it was the absolute wrong thing for me to take. I refused to take it
column8: and I never saw Dr. Sophy again after that. Towards the end of the month
column9: my mother invited Maddy and Mo Humpreys over for dinner. Mother had recently been reconnecting with her old friend Mo. Maddy had just graduated from USC
column10: a university renowned for its abundance of spoiled
column11: bratty students who partied all the time
column12: very similar to UCSB. I often call USC the ‚ÄúUniversity of Spoiled Cunts‚Äù
column13: just like I call UCSB the ‚ÄúUniversity of California‚Äôs Spoiled Brats‚Äù. Brilliant
column14: fitting nicknames! Before Maddy came
column15: I stalked her Facebook for a bit
column16: and I saw that she was the exact image of everything I hated in women. She was a popular
column17: spoiled USC girl who partied with her hot
column18: beautiful blonde-haired clique of friends. All of them looked like absolute cunts
column19: and my hatred for them all grew from each picture I saw on her profile. They were the kind of beautiful
column20: popular people who lived pleasurable lives and would look down on me as inferior scum
column21: never accepting me as one of them. They were my enemies. They represented everything that was wrong with this world. Maddy was my first friend in America. As a child
column22: I played with her as an equal. Now she was my enemy. I would take great delight in torturing and flaying her and every single one of her spoiled
column23: obnoxious evil friends. When she and her mother came to eat dinner with us
column24: I had to keep calm as I hobbled out of my room on my crutches to greet them. That relaxing month at mother‚Äôs was like the comfort before the storm. Once I go back to Santa Barbara
column25: fully recovered
column26: the final dark chapter of my life will commence. I dreaded what will come of it. After six weeks of hobbling about on crutches whenever I had to go somewhere
column27: I visited my orthopedist
column28: asking if it was finally time for me to walk without them. After examining my leg
column29: he agreed that I can proceed to a ‚Äúwalking cast‚Äù. This would enable me to limp around
column30: without crutches
column31: though I would have to use a cane. Though uncomfortable
column32: it was much
column33: much better than having to go about on those damnable crutches. When I got home
column34: I delightedly practiced moving around with this new setup. Before long
column35: my mother said I was now well enough to return to Santa Barbara. My new college classes were starting soon anyway
column36: though she didn‚Äôt know that I had only signed‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

column2: and that made me feel at least a small twinge of self-worth. It was quite surprising that he respected me so much
column3: since I had nothing in my life to boast about to him. He even asked me once if I had ever had a girlfriend
column4: and I angrily told him that the matter was none of his concern. I didn‚Äôt want to admit to him that girl‚Äôs thought I was a loser. If he found out about that
column5: he would respect me less. In order to boost his high opinion of me
column6: I often sugarcoated all of my early accomplishments
column7: such as telling him that I was an expert skateboarder and video game player. People having a high opinion of me is what I‚Äôve always wanted in life. It has always been of the utmost importance. This is why my life has been so miserable
column8: because no one has ever had a high opinion of me. My little brother Jazz was the only one who had such an opinion
column9: and that is why I enjoyed spending so much time with him
column10: despite my envy of his social advantages. During one of my frequent visits home in late Spring

This reunion was sparked by the political and philosophic conversations I had been having with Addison over Facebook. I decided to meet them at Philip‚Äôs house

column1: ‚Äépondered over the fact that I was now a teenager. I had a lovely time on this vacation. Our hotel suite was on one of the highest floors of the building
column2: and it had an exquisite deck that provided a view of the ocean. During the trip
column3: we toured around the island of Penang
column4: visited Georgetown
column5: went to a fun waterpark
column6: and had very delicious meals at many exotic restaurants. Just relaxing and watching movies at the hotel was a joy in itself. The vacation was so nice that I didn‚Äôt even miss my life at home. The three weeks flew by very fast
column7: and I cried a little when it was over. It was a good sadness. I celebrated my birthday again at father‚Äôs house on the night we returned to America. I was allowed to have my very first glass of beer for this celebration. I always thought of alcoholic drinks
column8: such as beer and wine
column9: as mysterious drinks that were forbidden to children like myself. Father would let me have only a small sip of wine from time to time. Having my first glass of beer felt like a big honor. For my present
column10: I got my first cell phone. During this era
column11: cell phones were like a rite of passage for kids my age. I always envied the kids who had a cell phone. John Jo had a silver Sprint phone with green lighting that I always coveted. To finally have a cell phone of my own made me feel so proud. My phone was a silver T-Mobile phone with blue lighting. I loved the satisfaction I felt when I opened it up and saw the pretty lights. 13 Years Old I enjoyed the rest of the summer as best as I could. On the first Planet Cyber session after being back from vacation
column12: I met up with John Jo. They had the new Warcraft 3 expansion available to play
column13: and the two of us tried it out. I had a sleepover with Charlie and Elijah
column14: and they introduced me to their friend Julian Ritz-Barr. Julian went to Topanga Elementary with us
column15: though he was two grades lower
column16: so I never knew him beforehand. I thought he was very cool
column17: but a bit stupid. We competed with each other at Planet Cyber. I continued to see him with Charlie and Elijah a few more times after that. Coincidentally
column18: Julian‚Äôs parents were friends with Rob Lemelson
column19: and I didn‚Äôt know this at the time. A few years down the line
column20: I would cross paths with Julian again at one of the Lemelson‚Äôs parties
column21: where I would spitefully envy him for being so confident with everyone. When the summer ended
column22: I cried a little. It was such a great summer. I went on a vacation
column23: I saw lots of friends
column24: played lots of games
column25: and enjoyed life to its fullest. Of course
column26: I didn‚Äôt know at the time that this was the last good summer in my life
column27: but I still cried... as I always do after a joyful‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

‚Äéat father‚Äôs house
column2: and father and Soumaya threw a party for me. They invited a few of my friends from Topanga Elementary
column3: though the only friend I remember being there was Philip and his younger brother Jeffrey. James was invited
column4: but he wasn‚Äôt able to make it. They also invited a few of Georgia‚Äôs friends
column5: which really annoyed me
column6: since it was my birthday
column7: and not Georgia‚Äôs. It was quite an eventful party
column8: and it took place in our backyard. My father hired a magician to perform tricks for everyone. 9 Years Old My ninth year was very interesting
column9: and I went through a lot of changes emotionally and intellectually. It was the year in which I matured to a point where I would start observing the world more conscientiously. Before I turned nine
column10: I was living life as a carefree child in a world that I thought was only good and pure. From this point onwards
column11: I would gradually discover more about the world and society. I would face problems and frustrations that I wouldn‚Äôt even think about before. My life would still be positive and bright
column12: however
column13: and I would live it to its fullest. The first frustration of the year
column14: which would remain for the rest of my life
column15: was the fact that I was very short for my age. As Fourth Grade started
column16: it fully dawned on me that I was the shortest kid in my class ‚Äì even the girls were taller than me. In the past
column17: I rarely gave a thought to it
column18: but at this stage I became extremely annoyed at how everyone was taller than me
column19: and how the tallest boys were automatically respected more. It instilled the first feelings of inferiority in me
column20: and such feelings would only grow more volatile with time. I desperately wanted to get taller
column21: and I read that playing basketball increases height. This sparked my brief interest in basketball
column22: and I would play it all the time during recess and lunch in the Upper. Most of the basketball courts were unused
column23: so I would play it by myself
column24: or with anyone who cared to join me. During my time at father‚Äôs
column25: I would spend hours playing basketball at father‚Äôs basketball court
column26: shooting hoop after hoop long into the evening
column27: and I also remember lying on the ground in the basketball court trying to stretch my body as much as I could in between basketball sessions. When I played basketball at school
column28: some boys would join me
column29: and when they did I saw that they were much better at the sport than me. I envied their ability to throw the ball at double the distance than I could. This made me realize that along with being short
column30: I was physically weak compared to other boys my age. Even boys younger than me were stronger. This vexed me to no end. My fourth grade classroom was located in the center area of the school
column31: and my teacher was named Mrs. Gill
column32: who had an assistant named Mr. Devine. Fourth‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

‚Äéto pass the time. I tried to relax and forget about all of the pain I had experienced in Santa Barbara. I wished there was something I could talk to my relatives about that would make them proud
column2: but there was nothing interesting or impressive about my life to talk about. I wished that I could show my grandma that I was thriving in Santa Barbara; that I had a girlfriend and I was enjoying a full and healthy life. But no
column3: that is something I was never able to do. I felt so ashamed of myself. My grandma
column4: Ah Mah
column5: gave me a gift of great value. After we exchanged all of the presents with the relatives
column6: Ah Mah presented me with a 22 karat golden necklace. It was very extravagant
column7: and it had been in her possession for a long time. Now it was mine. I wore it instantly and took a great liking to it. From then on
column8: I would wear it in every waking moment. I could have sold it for well over a thousand dollars
column9: but I never did. It was special to me. My favorite part of the trip was the breakfasts at the hotel. The hotel we stayed at was quite luxurious
column10: even by my standards. And the breakfasts... they were absolutely delicious. Every morning there was a buffet full of delectable choices
column11: such as crispy pork sausage
column12: croissants
column13: bacon
column14: ham
column15: roast potatoes
column16: all kinds of fruit
column17: and much more. On every one of those mornings
column18: I stuffed myself like there was no tomorrow. I was deprived of sex all my life
column19: so the only vice that I could derive a sense of pleasure from was eating delicious food. I took full advantage of that on this trip. My high metabolism prevented me from getting fat from it. Before the trip was over
column20: we all went on a tour through London. We were supposed to visit grandma Jinx in Smarden on that same day
column21: but grandma Jinx was in France at the time. We rented a comfortable Mercedes van that could fit eight people and made a long drive through the English countryside to the capital city of London. I didn‚Äôt remember much of the city from my previous visits
column22: so it was kind of like a new experience for me. I found the city to be very ugly in most areas
column23: but in contrast there were some attractive parts that were awash with beautiful architecture and a cultural atmosphere. Once we parked our van we set out on foot
column24: and sometimes took the Underground subway
column25: to explore all of the main attractions of the city. It was cold
column26: dreadfully cold
column27: but I bore it without much concern. Walking through the streets of London at night
column28: especially in Trafalgar Square and the area around Harrods was truly breathtaking
column29: or it would have been if I had a beautiful girlfriend by my side to experience it with me. It was the sort of place that one would go with a girlfriend
column30: and I had none.‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

‚Äédescribes my first day of Tenth Grade at Taft High School. It was a toxic nightmare. Every single second of it was agony. I continued to beg my parents to not make me go
column2: but it was to no avail. My father drove me there
column3: and I didn‚Äôt want to get out of his car. He almost had to drag me out. I somehow found the will to put one foot in front of the other and walk towards that awful
column4: ugly front building. The first week of Taft was living hell. I was bullied several times
column5: even though I didn‚Äôt know anyone there. After being so used to wearing a polo shirt with khaki pants as a school uniform at private schools
column6: I continued to dress like that even after leaving Crespi. I didn‚Äôt give any thought to how nerdy I looked. I was too withdrawn
column7: like a turtle tucked into his shell. I was still in the process of going through puberty at the time
column8: so I still looked and sounded like a ten-year-old. Such a persona attracted zero attention from girls
column9: of course
column10: but it did attract bullies like moths to a flame. I was completely and utterly alone. No one knew me or extended a hand to help me. I was an innocent
column11: scared little boy trapped in a jungle full of malicious predators
column12: and I was shown no mercy. Some boys randomly pushed me against the lockers as they walked past me in the hall. One boy who was tall and had blonde hair called me a ‚Äúloser‚Äù
column13: right in front of his girlfriends. Yes
column14: he had girls with him. Pretty girls. And they didn‚Äôt seem to mind that he was such an evil bastard. In fact

‚Äécouple of months after my birth
column2: I went on my first vacation. My parents took me on a boat to France. I was already a traveler! Of course
column3: I have no memories of this trip. My mother said that I cried a lot. At the time that I was born
column4: my mother and father were living in a house in London
column5: but shortly after my birth they decided to move to the countryside. We moved to a large house made of red brick in the county of Sussex
column6: with vast grass fields surrounding it. The house even had a name: The Old Rectory. This was where I spent my early childhood
column7: the first five years of my life
column8: and it was beautiful. The memories I have of this period are only memories of happiness and bliss. My father was a professional photographer at the time
column9: just in the stage of becoming a director. My mother gave up her nursing career to stay at home and look after me. My grandma on my mother‚Äôs side
column10: who I would call Ah Mah
column11: moved in with us to help out my mother. I would spend a lot of time with Ah Mah during these years. This was a time of discovery
column12: excitement
column13: and fun. I had just entered this new world
column14: and I knew nothing of the pain it would bring me later on. I enjoyed life with innocent bliss. I can remember playing in the fields and going on long walks with Ah Mah to pick berries. She would always warn me not to touch the stinging nettles that sometimes grew in our fields
column15: but my curiosity got the better of me
column16: and I got stung a few times. There was a swing in the back of our yard
column17: which I had many good times on. The first birthday I remember was my 3rd birthday. My parents threw a party for me in our field. I had a helicopter birthday cake. I can remember one of my friend‚Äôs parents cutting off the first piece and giving it to my friend. I threw a tantrum because I was expecting to get the first piece... It was my birthday after all. My father bought me a toy tractor that I could ride around in
column18: and I would play with it all the time after that. Sometime after my 3rd birthday
column19: we all went on a vacation to Malaysia
column20: my mother‚Äôs home country. I have only flashes of memory of that vacation. I enjoyed it very much. We visited a few of my mother‚Äôs relatives. For preschool
column21: I was enrolled at Dorsett House
column22: an upscale all-boys private school in the countryside
column23: near where we lived. I was forced to wear a uniform
column24: which I hated because I had to wear uncomfortable socks up to my knees. I was very nervous and I cried on my first day there. I can remember two friends I made by name
column25: George and David. I would always play in the sandpit with them. I didn‚Äôt like school at Dorsett House‚ÄéElliot Rodger‚Äé1

I remember going up there to skateboard all the way down; I rode my bicycle up there during my middle school years; I hiked up there with Max when he was staying at father‚Äôs house as an exchange student; I languished there in despair when I went on my lonely walks at the age of seventeen and eighteen; and now I was showing it to my grandma. ‚Äé     coming to ut has been a pretty crazy experience. my first week here i already felt like i was at home. it wasnt that way at the college i attended previously. that place took my two years to get comfortable. i think it has something to do with the fact that i have grown friendlier to most people. the one exception is my roommate drew. i don't know why we arent best friends
column2: but i think it has a lot to do wiht the fact that we come from two entirely distinct backgrounds. he was the son of a cop
column3: whos prents got divorced
column4: and i was the son of a librarian
column5: who parents always talked about getting divorced. also
column6: here women seem to enjoy my preasance more
column7: i think that has a lot to do with my radical overhaul of my diet and exercise routine. one girl in particular is named vanessa. she is an entirely charming girl
column8: with a body to die for
column9: but i don't know.  how do we trully know we found the right one
column10: is it that divine spark that fills our soul thew first time we lay eyes on a person or is it a familiarity that stems from shared time with someone that eventually turns to amore?  unfortunantly i was cursed with finding love at frist sight with my fist girl friend. but being as young as we were
column11: i cnat help but think what would hve happened if we would have met later. on this subject i have often wondered about my parents
column12: i have a vague notion that theirs was a marraige of convience. i overheard one day that the last time they had sex was the night i was concieved?!?!!??! i don't now what that means
column13: just came up. this i think is the point of life. the wondering
column14: lust filled age we are in now
column15: followed by silent impotent contemplation. no wonder why all people in power are old and white. they don't have the drive in their hearts to find romantic ideals the way youth does. in a sense i think i am an idealist. i like to see the glass as half full
column16: but usually only with people i care about. this deals alot with social conciense. what a terrible thing to have. all of us who are educated are confronted evertday with the absurdities of our goals. i want to have a nice house and car for my family and i want to help the world. but would i accept lesser for those i love to compensate those i do not know?!?!?!  off course not
column17: yet high minded individuals lambast me when they are doing the exact smae thing. so in the end what does one do
column18: pursue their own intersts or bang ones head against the system. i think it has to be a mixure of the two. because the more one knows
column19: the more one knows that nothing in this world is blakc and white. tragis heroes‚Äéstream of consciousness‚Äé0

‚Äéwell it's 13 min. into my writing and somehow i erased everything that i had. sorry. anyhow where was i.  I wonder if i should stop hanging around with sisters this semester. That would be really hard I don't think i could do it. I need to be around them. I wonder who i like the most right now. Is it abby? Yobel? still caroline?  I wonder if abby would ever hang out with me like one on one. I'm pretty sure yobel would
column2: but she's so young. It doesn't matter to me really but to other people it's such a big deal. I wish all that stuff i had written hadn't of disappeared. what did i do to make that happen?  now the reader's going to think i was goofing off while i should've been writing and made the whole disappearing thing up. i wonder if it'll let me go on for more that 20 minutes. why would it have a fishish button if you were supposed to write for the entire 20 minutes. do some people write shorter. I want to find out but i don't want to push the button cause i may not be able to go back. I wish i had't burned my tongue yesterday. I hate it when i burn my tongue. I wonder where yobel is. I'm starting to get if for her i'm afraid. And when i get it i get it bad. can't i just go one semester without liking somebody?  time's almost up. well i guess i'll contunue writing a little longer since my first part got erased. how did yobel's legs get so muscular. They're awesome. I like the way they make her walk. I wonder if i should've sat by her on the bus today. We could've talked for twice as long. I'm glad i waited for her afer i got off the bus. It probably hinted to her that i like her though. nobody else knows that. should i keep it that way. can i keep it that way?  i hope so. It was horrible last semester with caroline. everybody knew. I hate that. i got to keep this one in my head. in my heart. Maybe i can teach her volleyball and she could teach me soccer. she seemed pretty impressed when i told her about my volleyball class. I wonder if she likes me. Maybe she thinks i'm too old. I don't look old though. I bet peter hogan looks older than me
column3: and he's just a year older than yobel. If anything happens that wouldn't be too much of a problem. I wonder hwere peter hongan is now. why did he disappenar after he got his breakfast?  well if i'm not going bowling i guess i can spend money on breakfast. column1: ‚ÄéIt was a really humid day today while I was walking around on campus
column2: but now since I'm at home looking out my window
column3: I see so many people coming home from campus
column4: getting off the bus with water bottles. I realized that Texas weather is crazy. At one point it's humid and then the next it is super hot. It's nice and clear now. I'm sure its not that hot because it's going to be 6 pretty soon and it cools down around this time. Earlier today I thought it was going to rain. I really don't like walking around in the rain. My first day of class at UT it rained and I didn't know where I was going I was really miserable. Before you get home you get soaking wet and then the AC units are on all around campus so your freeze through your classes. Sometimes I wonder how the students at UT feel away from home. I know that my first semester here I was extremely homesick. I hated living away from home even though I was only 30 minutes away. I lived in an apt my first year and I regret the fact that I didn't live in a dorm because I would have gotten to meet so many more people that way. But then again I liked living in an apartment because I had my own space. I didn't have to share a bathroom with anyone and it sure wasn't a crowded community with only two people in 1200 square feet area. My freshman year I didn't meet very many people
column5: but my second year I was introduced to so many more people and I started enjoying college rather than just staying at home and reading or doing homework and watching tv. There's been a big change from then and now. I actually like to stay in austin instead of going home on the weekends because I always have something to do. At home I get bored very easily. Of course I still miss my parents but its so different here with all my friends. All kinds of crazy actions go on. College life it super busy. This semester I feel is going to fly by. Before I know it I will be on Thanksgiving break then studying for finals and then home again in two weeks after that. Sometimes I feel like I need to be more active in school programs and associations. I never have time though. Something is always coming up. I have a load of homework or a test coming up that stops me from attending these functions
